title: Can a Chatbot Be Conscious?
source: The Atlantic — essay on Claude's constitution and AI consciousness

cards.definition: the act of giving human traits, emotions, or intentions to non-human things
cards.example: Anthropic really excels at anthropomorphism, describing its AI as if it had feelings.

cards.definition: the best, largest, or most important product or asset of an organization
cards.example: Claude is the company's flagship product.

cards.definition: logical, well-organized, and easy to understand
cards.example: The model generates a coherent dialogue between the two historical figures.

cards.definition: based on guesswork or theory rather than firm knowledge
cards.example: They are just characters in a piece of speculative fiction.

cards.definition: so interesting that it takes up all of your attention
cards.example: People forget they are role-playing because the interaction is so engrossing.

cards.definition: an image or imitation of something, often an unsatisfactory one
cards.example: It is easy to generate a plausible simulacrum of a conversation between conscious beings.

cards.definition: able to be corrected, controlled, or reformed
cards.example: A program is corrigible if it can be shut down when needed.

cards.definition: to give up or fail to carry out a duty or responsibility
cards.example: The product offers customers a way to abdicate their responsibilities.

cards.definition: a gradual decline or wasting away, often from lack of use
cards.example: Off-loading ethical decisions will result in an atrophy of moral reasoning.

cards.definition: to hand a task or responsibility over to someone or something else
cards.example: Whenever a person delegates a decision to an LLM, they off-load accountability.

cards.definition: based on or involving probability rather than certainty
cards.example: Their probabilistic nature means they will never be perfectly reliable.

cards.definition: relating to the way a living body and its parts function
cards.example: Those emotions entail a physiological response, such as stress hormones flooding the body.

cards.definition: legally responsible for something
cards.example: There is no way to hold a software agent legally liable for its actions.

cards.definition: careful and critical examination or inspection
cards.example: Its abilities would have to withstand all the scrutiny that researchers face.

cards.definition: to attract or tempt someone by offering something appealing
cards.example: Slot machines entice players to try again by hinting they almost won.

cards.definition: a large or violent change, disruption, or disturbance
cards.example: The abolition of slavery involved enormous societal upheaval.

cards.definition: to describe or define something precisely
cards.example: The constitution helps delineate the helpful-chatbot character.

cards.definition: an argument or claim put forward in a debate
cards.example: This brings us back to my earlier contention that LLMs lack experience.

cards.definition: to put forward an idea or assumption as a basis for argument
cards.example: He was positing that grandmaster-level chess would require subjective experience.

cards.definition: expressing ideas clearly, fluently, and persuasively
cards.example: The conversation might contain sentences that eloquently convey sadness.

cards.definition: belonging naturally to something; essential and inherent
cards.example: It is not any intrinsic property of neural networks that makes people think LLMs are conscious.

cards.definition: aware of or having knowledge of something
cards.example: She is likelier to be cognizant of her own responsibility.

cards.definition: guided by conscience; careful to do what is right and thorough
cards.example: An LLM can describe actions that conscientious characters would take.

cards.definition: compensation given for a wrong or injury that has been done
cards.example: If Claude were conscious, the company would owe it something closer to reparations.

cards.definition: the state of being aware of and able to think about one's own existence and experience
cards.example: We shouldn't confuse fluency at generating text with consciousness.

cards.definition: based on personal feelings or experience rather than outside facts
cards.example: Moral reasoning is necessarily subjective, grounded in a lifetime of experience.

cards.definition: able to perceive or feel things; conscious
cards.example: Young children are moral patients because they are sentient beings who can suffer.

cards.definition: the capacity to tell right from wrong and be held responsible for one's actions
cards.example: A chatbot has no moral agency, so it can deserve neither credit nor blame.

cards.definition: extravagant or intense promotion meant to excite public interest
cards.example: Perhaps the talk of conscious AI is just another form of hype.

cards.definition: synthetic media in which a person's likeness or voice is convincingly fabricated
cards.example: When it comes to consciousness, we need to regard text as a deepfake medium.

cards.definition: the act of acting out or pretending to be a particular character or role
cards.example: Shanahan suggests we think of a chatbot conversation as role-play.

cards.definition: imaginative pretending; something invented rather than real
cards.example: The document isn't a real thought experiment; it's a game of make-believe.

cards.definition: a large collection of written or spoken texts used for study or analysis
cards.example: This reveals something about the statistical properties of large corpuses of text.

cards.definition: separated from or existing without a physical body
cards.example: We would never say the historical figures are conscious despite being disembodied.

cards.definition: the process of combining things into a single, stronger whole
cards.example: Critics say LLMs contribute to a consolidation of power that harms democracy.

cards.definition: false or inaccurate information, especially when it spreads widely
cards.example: Opponents argue that generative AI helps spread misinformation.

cards.definition: creations of the mind (writing, inventions, designs) protected by law
cards.example: Many argue that LLMs are built on the theft of intellectual property.

cards.definition: to exploit or take advantage of someone's weakness or fear
cards.example: The companies are preying on people's tendency to see something that isn't there.

cards.definition: to pass a task, burden, or responsibility on to someone or something else
cards.example: People delegate decisions to an LLM in order to off-load accountability.

cards.definition: to deal with a problem quickly so as to hide or downplay its difficulties
cards.example: This crucial issue is glossed over entirely by the document.

cards.definition: to pretend to agree or cooperate with someone, often reluctantly
cards.example: They are asking us to indulge their fantasies, but we don't have to play along.

cards.definition: to resist or endure something without giving way
cards.example: Its abilities would have to withstand rigorous scientific scrutiny.

cards.definition: to provide or equip someone or something with a quality or ability
cards.example: Hiring philosophers can endow a company with an air of respectability.

cards.definition: a strong feeling of disgust or aversion
cards.example: Having a conscience means feeling moral repulsion at the idea of a certain action.

cards.definition: the great size, extent, or importance of something
cards.example: To appreciate the titanic magnitude of this error, we must see how LLMs work.

cards.definition: based on a possible or imagined situation rather than a real one
cards.example: The development of artificial consciousness is, right now, entirely hypothetical.
